title: Controlled optical properties vitreous enamel composition for electrosurgical tool
abstract: A vitreous enamel coating for an electrosurgical metal cutting blade, in which incident light striking the vitreous enamel coating is diffusely reflected or absorbed, and the vitreous enamel coating exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 100 gloss units as measured according to ASTM D523-14, Standard Test Method for Specular Gloss. The coating reduces glare from light sources such as a nearby plasma-mediated discharge, operating theater lights or lights provided on an electrosurgery apparatus. The coating may also lessen interference with markers, sensors or other instruments designed to measure light emitted by or passing through nearby tissue such as by transillumination.
claims: Claims Claims: 1. An article comprising: (a) an electrosurgical cutting blade comprising a metal electrode, and (b) a visible vitreous enamel coating on at least a portion of the metal electrode, wherein incident light striking the vitreous enamel coating is diffusely reflected or absorbed, and the vitreous enamel coating exlaibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 100 gloss units as measured according to ASTM D523-14, Standard Test Method for Specular Gloss. 2. A visible diffusely reflective or absorptive vitreous enamel coating formed from glass frit, wherein the coating is disposed on a metal electrode for an electrosurgical cutting blade and exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 100 gloss units as measured according to ASTM D523-14, Standard Test Method for Specular Gloss, 3. A method comprising providing a vitreous enamel precursor, applying the vitreous enamel precursor onto at least a portion of a metal electrode suitabl e for use as an electrosurgical cutting blade, and firing the vitreous enamel precursor to form a visible diffusely reflective or absorptive vitreous enamel coating that exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 100 gloss units as measured according to ASTM D523-14, Standard Test Method for Specular Gloss. 4. A method comprising intermittently supplying radiofrequency energy to an electrosurgical cutting blade having a visible diffusely reflective or absorptive vitreous enamel coating that exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 100 gloss units as measured according to ASTM D523-14, Standard Test Method for Specular Gloss, to create a plasma-mediated discharge, 5. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 80 gloss units, 6. An article according to claim 1 5 coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 40 gloss units. 7. An article according to claim 1 , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating exhibits a 60Â° gloss value less than 20 gloss units. 8. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating is sufficiently diffusely reflective or absorptive so that the underlying metal electrode is not visible through the coating under typical indoor illumination. 9. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating is sufficiently diffusely reflective or absorptive so that a specular second surface reflection from the underlying metal electrode is not visible through the coating during plasma operation. 10. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating diffusely reflects or absorbs light in a visible light wavelength band of interest. 11. An article according to claim I , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating diffusely reflects or absorbs light in an infrared light wavelength hand of interest. 12. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein a visible outer surface of the vitreous enamel coating is etched or otherwise roughened so that it will not specularly reflect incident light. 13. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating contains sufficient refractive inorganic pigment so that the coating will absorb or scatter incident light. 14. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4. wherein the vitreous enamel coating contains a non-infrared- absorptive inorganic pigment. 15. An article according to claim 1 , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel is an amorphous glass composition. 16. Ai article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel is a glass-ceramic composition having a crystalline phase. 17. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating contains sirfficient crystallinity so that the coating will absorb or scatter incident light. 18. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating contains at least 20 volume percent of a crystalline phase. 19. An article according to claim 1 , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the metal electrode is etched or otherwise roughened so that incident light passing into the vitreous enamel coating will not be specularly reflected by the metal electrode. 20. An article according to claim 1 , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or clai 4, wherein a non-specularly reflective coating is applied between the metal electrode and the vitreous enamel coating, so that incident light passing into the vitreous enamel coating will not be specularly reflected by the metal electrode, 21. An article according to claim 1 , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating discourages reflection of visible or other light in colors that might interfere with markers, sensors or other instruments that measure light emitted by or passing through nearby tissue. 22. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating has a thickness of about 50 Î¼Î·Î¹ to about 100 Î¼Î·Î¹. 23. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating contains sufficient pigment or colorant, or has sufficient crystallinity, so that the metal electrode is not visible through the coating under typical indoor illumination. 24. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel coating contains sufficient pigment or colorant, or has sufficient crystallinity, so that a specular second surface reflection from the metal electrode is not visible through the coating during plasma operation. 25. An article according to claim 1, coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel comprises an aluminoborosilicate glass, 26. An article according to claim 1 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel is formed from a glass frit comprising, as molar percentages: Si0 2 30-50%, B 2 0 3 0.5- 15%, Al 2 <Â¾ 0.5-10%, SrO 5-30%, CaO 5-30%, and ZnO 0,5-20%. 27. An article according to claim 1 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the vitreous enamel is formed from a glass frit comprising Si0 2 , B2O3, AS2O3, and optionally one or more of SrO, BaO, CaO, MgO, ZnO, Na 2 0 5 K 2 0 or a combination thereof. 28. A method according to claim 3, wherein the vitreous enamel precursor includes glass frit and at least one binder. 29. An article according to claim 1 , coating according to claim 2 or method according to claim 3 or claim 4, wherein the metal electrode is titanium, tantalum, molybdenum, tungsten, stainless steel, or an alloy thereof. 30. An article according to claim 1, further comprising an insulated handle attached to the electrosurgical cutting blade and housing at least one conductor that can connect the metal electrode to a radiofrequenc energy power supply.
